title: Glossary of Terms
description: Technical terms and definitions used throughout The Seed

ssid.definition: The name of a wireless network that devices use to identify and connect to it. Each WiFi network broadcasts its SSID so devices can find it.

bssid.definition: The MAC address of the access point's radio. Used to uniquely identify a specific access point when multiple APs share the same SSID.

rssi.definition: A measurement of the power level of a received wireless signal, typically expressed in dBm. Higher values (closer to 0) indicate stronger signals.

snr.definition: The ratio between the desired signal strength and background noise, measured in dB. Higher SNR values indicate cleaner, more reliable connections.

channel.definition: A specific frequency range within the 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz band used for wireless communication. Proper channel selection reduces interference.

channelWidth.definition: The frequency range used by a WiFi channel (20, 40, 80, or 160 MHz). Wider channels provide higher throughput but are more susceptible to interference.

802.11ax.definition: The IEEE standard for high-efficiency wireless LANs, offering improved performance in dense environments through OFDMA and MU-MIMO technologies.

802.11be.definition: The latest IEEE wireless standard featuring 320 MHz channels, 4096-QAM modulation, and multi-link operation for extremely high throughput.

mcs.definition: An index value that defines the modulation type, coding rate, and number of spatial streams used in a WiFi transmission. Higher MCS values indicate faster potential speeds.

phy.definition: The lowest layer of the network stack that handles the actual transmission and reception of raw data bits over the wireless medium.

wpa2.definition: A security protocol using AES encryption to protect wireless networks. The standard security method for most WiFi networks.

wpa3.definition: The latest WiFi security standard offering stronger encryption, protection against offline dictionary attacks, and improved security for open networks.

dfs.definition: A mechanism that allows WiFi devices to use 5 GHz channels shared with radar systems by detecting and avoiding radar signals.

tdr.definition: A diagnostic technique that sends electrical pulses through a cable and analyzes reflections to detect faults, opens, shorts, and cable length.

dhcp.definition: A network protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters to devices on a network.

dns.definition: The system that translates human-readable domain names (like example.com) into IP addresses that computers use to identify each other.

mtu.definition: The largest packet size that can be transmitted over a network without fragmentation. Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes.

cidr.definition: A method for allocating IP addresses and routing using variable-length subnet masking (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24).

vlan.definition: A logical grouping of network devices that can communicate as if on the same physical network, regardless of their physical location.

arp.definition: A protocol used to map IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network, enabling devices to find each other.

icmp.definition: A network protocol used for diagnostic and error reporting, including ping and traceroute functionality.

ttl.definition: A value in IP packets that limits their lifespan, decremented at each router hop. Prevents packets from circulating indefinitely.

oui.definition: The first three bytes of a MAC address that identify the manufacturer of a network device.

mac.definition: A unique hardware identifier assigned to network interfaces. Also refers to the data link layer protocol that controls access to the network medium.

lldp.definition: A vendor-neutral protocol used by network devices to advertise their identity, capabilities, and neighbors on a local network.

cdp.definition: A Cisco proprietary protocol for discovering information about directly connected Cisco devices, including model, IP address, and capabilities.

edp.definition: A protocol used by Extreme Networks devices to discover neighboring network equipment and share device information.

ndp.definition: An IPv6 protocol that performs functions similar to ARP, including address resolution and router discovery.

snmp.definition: A protocol for collecting and organizing information about managed devices on IP networks and modifying that information to change device behavior.

dicom.fullName: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine
dicom.definition: The international standard for transmitting, storing, and sharing medical imaging information. Used by CT scanners, MRI machines, and PACS systems.

aeTitle.definition: A unique identifier for a DICOM application or device on a network. Used to establish associations between DICOM systems.

cEcho.definition: A DICOM verification service that tests connectivity between two DICOM nodes. Similar to a network ping for DICOM systems.

pacs.definition: A medical imaging system that provides storage and access to medical images from multiple modalities.

rtsp.definition: A network protocol used to control streaming media servers, commonly used for IP security cameras and video surveillance systems.

iperf.definition: A widely-used tool for measuring network throughput between two endpoints. Supports TCP and UDP testing with various configuration options.

speedtest.definition: A test that measures download speed, upload speed, and latency to determine internet connection performance.

latency.definition: The time delay between sending a request and receiving a response, typically measured in milliseconds (ms). Lower is better.

jitter.definition: The variation in latency over time. High jitter causes problems for real-time applications like VoIP and video conferencing.

throughput.definition: The actual rate of successful data transfer over a network, measured in bits per second (bps, Mbps, Gbps).

packetLoss.definition: The percentage of packets that fail to reach their destination. Even small amounts can significantly impact application performance.

ttfb.definition: The time from when a request is made until the first byte of the response is received. Indicates server responsiveness.
